feedback.text: Dear Members of the European Parliament and Commission, I am writing to respectfully urge you to strengthen the Digital Fairness Act by including clear protections for consumers like me who purchase digital media. Im increasingly concerned that digital products I legally buyespecially video games and moviescan later be taken away or made unusable, even when there's no technical reason for it. This undermines my rights as a consumer and calls into question whether digital ownership actually exists. Ive personally experienced cases where Ive bought a game that later became completely unplayable, simply because the publisher shut down its servers. One of the most troubling examples was Ubisofts decision to shut down The Crew, a racing game I and millions of others had paid for. Even though I owned the game and it functioned well, the publisher removed access for everyone by turning off the servers. There was no option for me to continue playing, even in single-player mode. It felt like my purchase had been revoked without consent. This is deeply unfairespecially for games that dont truly require constant internet connections. What Id like to see in EU law is a requirement for publishers and developers to offer an alternative before removing access to any digital game or service Ive paid for. If a game doesnt need live service to work, it should be updated to function offline. And if it does need online services, then the law should compel publishers to let the community take overwhether that means running private servers or offering a license for continued access. This way, games I buy wont disappear just because a company decides to sunset them. Unfortunately, this issue goes beyond video games. I was shocked to see Sony remove access to hundreds of movies and TV shows that people had purchased from the PlayStation Storetitles like Paddington and The Hunger Games. Even though customers had paid for permanent access, Sony deleted them from user libraries because of licensing changes. If a company can take away movies I bought and tell me I cant watch them anymore, what did I actually pay for? To me, this is a clear sign that stronger rules are needed. I understand that the current Digital Content Directive gives consumers some rightslike refunds if content stops working, and a vague promise of reasonable accessbut it doesnt go far enough. In practice, companies are still able to pull products out from under me with little or no warning, and without offering a fallback option. This isnt right. What I hope the Digital Fairness Act will do is establish clear obligations around digital purchases. If I buy a game or a movie, I should be able to keep using it, unless I was clearly informed otherwise from the start. If service needs to end, the publisher should either give me a version that still works offline, offer a full refund, or provide tools so the community can maintain access. The current system, where companies can just delete what Ive paid for, doesnt respect me as a paying customer. To be clear, Im not asking for unlimited access to live services or online multiplayer. I just want a fair guarantee that what I buy will remain usableespecially if its a single-player game or personal media like movies. Digital ownership should mean something. And I believe this is something that European law can and should protect. Thank you for taking the time to read my feedback. I appreciate your work on making digital markets more fair and consumer-friendly, and I sincerely hope that these concerns will be reflected in the final version of the Digital Fairness Act.
